title: REPUBLIC OF KENYA
text:
REPUBLIC OF KENYA
IN THE TAX APPEALS TRIBUNAL AT NAIROBI
TAT APPEAL NO. E142 OF 2025
AINUSHAMSI CONSTRUCTION AND
TRANSPORTERS LIMITED…………………….………….….……………...…
APPELLANT
-VS-
COMMISSIONER OF INVESTIGATIONS & ENFORCEMENT.............
….RESPONDENT
JUDGMENT
BACKGROUND
1. The Appellant is a construction company incorporated in Kenya under the
Companies Act.
2. The Respondent is the principal officer appointed under section 13 of the
Kenya Revenue Authority Act. The Kenya Revenue Authority is an agency
of the Government of Kenya mandated with the duty of collecting and
receiving all tax revenue, and the administration and enforcement of all
tax laws set out in Parts 1& 2 of the First Schedule to the Act, for purposes
of assessing, collecting, and accounting for all tax revenues in accordance
with those laws.
3. The issue in dispute in this appeal arose when the Respondent carried out
investigations into the Appellant’s tax affairs to ascertain its tax liabilities,
leading to the issuance of a tax assessment dated 21st August 2024.
Judgment TAT No. E142 of 2025 (Ainushamsi Construction and Transporters Limited vs
Commissioner of Investigations and Enforcement)
Page 1 of 15

4. The Appellant objected to the said demand vide its late Objection letter
dated 13th November 2024 and the Respondent issued its decision vide a
letter dated 24th December 2024.
5. Dissatisfied with the Respondent's decision, the Appellant lodged its
appeal via a Notice of Appeal dated 23rd January 2025.
THE APPEAL
6. The Appellant, in its Memorandum of Appeal dated and filed on 12th
February 2025 set out its grounds of appeal as follows:
a) THAT the Respondent erred in fact and in law by failing to
appreciate and take into consideration the Matching Concept
principle. The Respondent indeed failed to take into consideration
the Appellant's recognized revenues and their related expenses in
the accounting period when determining the income tax payable.
b) THAT the Respondent erred in law and fact by failing to consider
non-sales and non-income items such as director deposits, loans,
and reversals when computing taxable income, thereby overstating
the Appellant’s tax liability.
c)THAT the Respondent erred in law and fact by failing to adhere to
the principle of procedural fairness, thereby violating the
Appellant’s right to fair administrative action as guaranteed under
Article 47 of the Constitution of Kenya.
d) THAT the Respondent erred in law and fact by issuing an
assessment that is excessive, unreasonable, and lacks a rational
basis, contrary to the principle established in Robert Ayisi v.
Kenya Revenue Authority and Another, NRB Petition No.
412 of 2016 [2018] eKLR.
Judgment TAT No. E142 of 2025 (Ainushamsi Construction and Transporters Limited vs
Commissioner of Investigations and Enforcement)
Page 2 of 15

e) THAT the Respondent erred in law and fact by disregarding the
reconciliations of banking and sales prepared in accordance with
VAT returns filed by the Appellant, which confirm that all sales were
duly declared for VAT purposes.
f) THAT the Respondent erred in law and fact by failing to distinguish
non-taxable items in its banking analysis and arbitrarily subjecting
the total gross bank deposits to VAT and Income Tax.
g) THAT the Respondent erred in law and fact by failing to explain how
the demanded amount of Kshs. 79,273,665.6 was arrived at,
despite the initial investigation findings on 23rd July 2024 indicating
a demand of Kshs. 14,795,045.
h) THAT the Respondent erred in law and fact by issuing a demand on
24th September 2024 that significantly increased the tax liability
without any attempt to explain the variance from prior demands
dated 23rd July 2024, 21st August 2024, and 15th October 2024.
i) THAT the Respondent erred in law and fact by issuing a demand
and objection decision that violates the Appellant's rights to fair
administrative action and legitimate expectation.
APPELLANT’S CASE
7. The Appellant supported its appeal with its Statement of Facts dated and
filed on 12th January 2026. It did not file submissions within the set
deadlines in this matter and was, as such, directed by the Tribunal to
proceed on the basis of its pleadings on 3rd February 2026.
8. The Appellant averred that it was served with letters dated 23rd July 2024,
21st August 2024 and 15th October 2024, all demanding about 14 million,
and it was thus baffled when the demand ballooned to Kshs. Kshs. 79,
273,665.65.
Judgment TAT No. E142 of 2025 (Ainushamsi Construction and Transporters Limited vs
Commissioner of Investigations and Enforcement)
Page 3 of 15

9. That the new figure has never been explained, but it can be discerned
that the Respondent conducted a bank analysis of the Appellant's account
at Equity Bank for the period 2016-2020 and proceeded to determine the
net banking income by adjusting for non-income items, VAT, and opening
and closing debtors, ultimately arriving at the demanded figure.
10. That these figures from the bank constitute non-sale and non-income
items, and the same ought to have been adjusted in the determination of
the net banking.
11. It was the Appellant’s view under the circumstances that the
Respondent’s:
i. Banking analysis was flawed, erroneous, and incorrect as it
omitted several entries and overstated others.
ii.Actions in charging VAT and income tax on total gross bank
deposits without distinguishing non-sale items such as
director deposits, loans, and reversals constitute an abuse of
power.
iii. Actions were not in tandem with Article 210 of the
Constitution and relevant tax laws, as it issued assessments
that disregarded non-taxable items beyond its constitutional
and legislative mandate.
iv. Assessment was excessive, unreasonable, and lacked a
rational basis, contrary to the dicta in Robert Ayisi v. Kenya
Revenue Authority and Another, NRB Petition No. 412
of 2016 [2018] eKLR.
12. Additionally, the Appellant stated that its reconciliation of banking and
sales and the VAT returns filed confirm that all sales have been duly
declared for VAT purposes.
Judgment TAT No. E142 of 2025 (Ainushamsi Construction and Transporters Limited vs
Commissioner of Investigations and Enforcement)
Page 4 of 15

13. That the said reconciliation provides a detailed breakdown of the sales
revenue and corresponding banking transactions, ensuring that there are
no discrepancies or omissions.
14. That, contrary to the Respondent's assertion, it doesn’t have undeclared
sales that would warrant additional VAT liability.
15. That the Respondent ought to have considered all the Appellant's
expenses as demanded by the Matching Concept principle to get a holistic
view of its expenses.
Appellant’s Prayer
16. The Appellant prays for orders that the:
a.THAT this appeal be allowed.
b.THAT the Respondent’s Objection Decision dated 24th December
2024 be set aside in its entirety and the demanded amount be
vacated.
RESPONDENT'S CASE
17. The Respondent opposed this appeal with the support of its Statement of
Facts dated 14th March 2025 and Written Submissions dated 16th
September 2025.
18. The Respondent stated that it conducted various tests, including income
tax, VAT declarations, and income analysis of the banking deposits to
confirm that the Appellant was compliant with its tax obligations.
19. That it undertook an analysis of the Appellant’s bank statements from
Equity Bank and determined the gross deposits, from which it proceeded
to adjust for opening and closing debtors, VAT, and WHT in establishing
the taxable income.
Judgment TAT No. E142 of 2025 (Ainushamsi Construction and Transporters Limited vs
Commissioner of Investigations and Enforcement)
Page 5 of 15

20. That it subsequently compared the established income to the income
declared by the Appellant in their IT2C returns to establish undeclared
income.
21. That in determining the undeclared income for VAT, it adjusted exempt
supplies made by the Appellant as well as taxable declared sales.
22. And based on the aforementioned findings, it issued a tax assessment
through iTax amounting to Kshs. 87,314,650 on 21st August 2024.
23. The Respondent stated that it reviewed the grounds relied upon and the
supporting documents provided by the Appellant, and it granted the
Appellant an extension, as communicated vide its letter dated 28th
October 2024.
24. That it thereafter recomputed the Appellant’s tax liability and partially
allowed the Appellant’s objection vide its objection decision confirming
taxes amounting to Kshs. 51,620,647 from Kshs. 87,314,650.
25. The Respondent averred that in the appeal before this Tribunal, the
Appellant has not pointed out a single transaction or expense that the
Respondent did not take into account.
26. That on the contrary, the Respondent made necessary adjustments in
instances where there were sufficient supporting documents. For
example, where the Appellant was able to demonstrate that some of the
entries in the bank accounts were unpaid cheques, the same were
adjusted in accordance with the documents the Appellant provided.
27. It was its position that the Appellant ought to have annexed loan
statements and agreements in support of the loans, bank statements to
demonstrate interbank transfers, and director injections from the
directors’ accounts to support its allegations.
Judgment TAT No. E142 of 2025 (Ainushamsi Construction and Transporters Limited vs
Commissioner of Investigations and Enforcement)
Page 6 of 15

28. That the Appellant ought to have gone a step further to explain what
constitutes the other deposits in its account, which it termed non-trade
income/revenue.
29. The Respondent alleged that the Appellant has provided neither a single
document nor a breakdown or alternative reconciliation before this
Tribunal to demonstrate that indeed the assessment was wrong.
30. The Respondent stated that the Appellant solely submitted sample
purchase orders received from the United Nations High Commissioner for
Refugees (UNHCR), the Danish Refugee Council, the Norwegian Refugee
Council, and Peace Winds Japan to substantiate the claimed exempt
transactions with privileged organizations. However, the Appellant did not
make any attempt to explain or declare the income from the county
governments of Garissa and Vihiga.
31. It was its position that the Appellant was engaged throughout the
investigation process and was granted an opportunity to contest the
assessment, and in instances where the Appellant discharged the burden,
the Respondent made adjustments to favor the Appellant.
32. The Respondent posited that it treated the variances as under-declared
income and offered the Appellant an opportunity to issue a response and
avail documents in support of its position if, at all, it had a contrary view.
33. The Respondent emphasized that it had requested the Appellant for
documents, but the Appellant failed to avail the same, contrary to its
obligation of keeping records under Section 23 of the TPA and as was
stressed in the case of Boleyn International Ltd v. Commissioner of
Investigations and Enforcement, Nairobi TAT Appeal No. 55 of
2018.
34. That the Appellant had failed to discharge its burden of proof as was
expected of it under Section 56 of the TPA.
Judgment TAT No. E142 of 2025 (Ainushamsi Construction and Transporters Limited vs
Commissioner of Investigations and Enforcement)
Page 7 of 15

35. On whether the Respondent erred in raising additional assessments using
the Banking method, the Respondent submitted that it opted for the
Banking method and it was incumbent on the Appellant to demonstrate
that credits in its account were not deposits, and it failed to do so.
36. On whether the Appellant discharged the burden of proof in accordance
with Section 56(1) of the TPA and Section 30 of TATA, the Respondent
stated that the Appellant has not pointed out a single transaction or
expense that the Respondent did not take into account.
37. That as much as Section 15 of the ITA allows for the deduction of
expenses incurred in generating income, the Appellant must demonstrate
through supporting documents that it indeed incurred the expenses and
that the same were incurred in the process of generating income.
38. That the Respondent's assessment enjoys a presumption of correctness
unless demonstrated otherwise under Section 56(1) of the TPA 2015 as
was explained in Kenya Revenue Authority v. Maluki Kitili Mwendwa
[2021] eKLR.
39. The Respondent submitted that the Kshs. 14,795,045 the Appellant is
referring to was an assessment for one financial year, not the entire
assessment period starting from 2018 to 2023.
40. The Respondent submitted that the Appellant, having failed to discharge
its burden of demonstrating how the assessment was excessive through
proper documentation, it left the Respondent with no choice other than
confirming the assessment with respect to what was supported, as was
asserted in Alfred Kioko Muteti vs. Timothy Miheso & Another
[2015] eKLR.
ISSUES FOR DETERMINATION.
Judgment TAT No. E142 of 2025 (Ainushamsi Construction and Transporters Limited vs
Commissioner of Investigations and Enforcement)
Page 8 of 15

41. The Tribunal notes that this appeal has been reinstated for hearing on its
merits, vide directions of the High Court issued on 16th January 2026 and
which also set aside the previous decision of the Tribunal that had struck
out the appeal for incompetence.
42. In view of the above directions, the Tribunal has considered the parties'
pleadings, submissions, and documents submitted, and is of the view that
the substantive issues that has invited its determination are whether:
a) Whether the Tribunal can consider issues that were
never raised by the Appellant in its objection notice dated
20th February 2025.
b) Whether the Respondent was justified in applying
banking analysis to determine the Appellant’s tax
liability.
ANALYSIS AND DETERMINATION
43. The identified issues for determination shall be determined sequentially
as hereunder:
A. Whether the Tribunal can consider issues that were never
raised by the Appellant in its objection notice dated 20th
February 2025.
44. Section 13(6) of the TAT Act provides as follows:
“(6) The Appellant shall, unless the Tribunal orders
otherwise, be limited to the grounds stated in the
appeal or the documents to which the decision
relates. “Emphasis ours.
45. The jurisdiction of the Tribunal is thus limited to hearing and determining
disputes regarding issues that were raised before the Commissioner. The
Tribunal cannot entertain or consider new grounds of appeal or documents
Judgment TAT No. E142 of 2025 (Ainushamsi Construction and Transporters Limited vs
Commissioner of Investigations and Enforcement)
Page 9 of 15

that are introduced in a taxpayer’s memorandum of appeal if the same
were never raised or presented to the Commissioner to consider and
make a decision.
46. Indeed, hearing disputes that raise issues that were not canvassed before
the Commissioner would convert the role of the Tribunal to that of the
Commissioner instead of retaining its statutory role of only hearing
appeals that emanate from decisions made by the Commissioner.
47. When presented with a similar situation, the High Court in the recent
decision of Commissioner of Investigation & Enforcement v
Wamunyinyi [2026] KEHC 379 (KLR) has held as follows”
“Guided by the above, I find that the Tribunal erred in law by
admitting and relying on evidence that was not presented
before the Commissioner at the objection stage. This error goes
to the heart of the objection review process and undermines the
Commissioner’s statutory mandate to assess tax based on
information provided by the taxpayer.”
48. The Appellant’s objection dated 13th novmber2024 was premised on the
following issues:
a)The Commissioner did not consider its workings and documents.
b)The Commissioner’s assessment disregarded nontaxable items.
c) The Appellant was working exclusively for not for profit
organizations, and hence its 100%profit margin was disregarded.
d)The assessments for 2016 to 2018 were beyond the 5-year
period.
e)The Appellant did not hold any underdeclared sales for VA
purposes.
f) The actions of the Commissioner contravened Article 47 of the
Constitution and the Fair Administration Act.
Judgment TAT No. E142 of 2025 (Ainushamsi Construction and Transporters Limited vs
Commissioner of Investigations and Enforcement)
Page 10 of 15

49. However, at the appeal stage before the Tribunal, the Appellant has now
introduced a new ground stating that the demand of Kshs 79,273,665,65
is at variance with prior demands of Kshs 14,795,045.
50. This ground is clearly new; it was never raised at the objection stage to
cause a response or consideration by the Respondent. As guided by
Section 13(6) of the TAT Act and the Wamunyinyi decision (supra), the
Tribunal is obliged to resist attempts, however attractive or meritorious, to
consider issues that were never raised before the Commissioner during
objection proceedings, lest it morph itself from an appellate to a trial tribunal,
which is unknown to the law.
51. It is for this reason that the Tribunal shall not consider grounds 7 and 9 as
raised by the Appellant in its Memorandum of Appeal dated 12th February
2025.
B. Whether the Respondent was justified in applying
banking analysis to determine the Appellant’s tax
liability.
52. It is trite that banking analysis is a legal model of tax assessment.
Nevertheless, it should only be used in hopeless cases where the
evidence provided by the taxpayer is so inept that it is difficult for the
Commissioner to determine the actual tax liability of such a taxpayer. In
such cases, the Commissioner is allowed to apply its best judgment under
Section 31(1) of the TPA by applying banking analysis to determine the
tax liability of that taxpayer. The said Section 31 (1) of the TPA provides
as follows:
“(1) Subject to this section, the Commissioner may amend an
assessment (referred to in this section as the “original
assessment”) by making alterations or additions from the
available information and to the best of the Commissioner's
Judgment TAT No. E142 of 2025 (Ainushamsi Construction and Transporters Limited vs
Commissioner of Investigations and Enforcement)
Page 11 of 15

judgment, to the original assessment of a taxpayer for a
reporting period to ensure that—
(a)…”
53. The above position was asserted by the Tribunal in Judgment Tat E931
of 2023 Superserve Limited vs. Commissioner of Investigation
and Enforcement, where it stated as follows:
“Moreover, the Appellant ought to apply its best judgment
only in cases where the Appellant has failed to supply it
with the relevant documents or in cases where the
material placed before it is insufficient. In the case of the
latter, it shall be required to identify and call for the
missing documents. "It is only when such documents have
not been supplied that it can resort to its best judgment.”
54. The issue that falls in this appeal, therefore, is whether the evidence or
documents provided by the Appellant were sufficient to help the
Commissioner determine its tax liability without resorting to the banking
analysis method.
55. The Appellant stated that it provided and indeed attached the following
documents to its Objection letter dated 13th November 2024:
a)Banking analysis
b)Letters of contract awards
c) Loan agreements.
56. The Respondent's response to these issues in the objection decision was
as follows:
a)That whereas it received loan agreements confirming that the
Appellant worked for non-profit organizations, a review of its
banking analysis revealed substantial deposits from the county
governments and parastatals, which were not explained.
Judgment TAT No. E142 of 2025 (Ainushamsi Construction and Transporters Limited vs
Commissioner of Investigations and Enforcement)
Page 12 of 15

b)That the Appellant did not provide a schedule of deposits from
the four companies that were related to it, and no adjustments
were made on those deposits.
c)The Appellant averred but failed to provide evidence to show the
funds that were deposited to its account by directors when the
company was low on funds.
57. The Appellant is legally required to provide relevant documents when
filing its objection. It was thus obliged to provide documents explaining
the cash deposit in its bank account, which had been paid by county
governments and parastatals. An explanation of the source and reasons
for these deposits, which had not been accounted for in its self-
assessment, would have helped it discharge its burden placed on it under
Section 30 of the TAT Act, which provides as follows:
“In a proceeding before the Tribunal, the Appellant has the
burden of proving—
(a) where an appeal relates to an assessment, that
the assessment is excessive; or
(b) in any other case, that the tax decision should not
have been made or should have been made
differently.”
58. Additionally, the Appellant was also under a duty to provide a schedule or
an explanation regarding the schedule of deposits from the four
companies that he admitted to have been related to it and from where it
had sought adjustments on the basis of the said relation.
59. Finally, the Appellant averred that some deposits made into its bank
account were done by directors when the company was low on funds.
Once again, it was obligated to provide evidence of at least a CR12 to
show that these deposits were indeed made by directors and possibly an
Judgment TAT No. E142 of 2025 (Ainushamsi Construction and Transporters Limited vs
Commissioner of Investigations and Enforcement)
Page 13 of 15

agreement showing that the directors were advancing money to the
enterprise. This was not done.
60. Having failed to provide documentation or a reasonable explanation to
address these three issues, it is the finding of the Tribunal that the
Respondent did not fall into error when it opted to apply best judgment to
determine the Appellant’s tax liability.
61. The Tribunal’s finding aligns with its previous decision in JUDGMENT TAT
E931 OF 2023 Superserve Limited vs. Commissioner of
Investigation and Enforcement, where it stated as follows: on when
the Commissioner can apply its best judgment.
“Moreover, the Appellant ought to apply its best judgment
only in cases where the Appellant has failed to supply it
with the relevant documents or in cases where the
material placed before it is insufficient." In the case of the
latter, it shall be required to identify and call for the
missing documents. It is only when such documents have
not been supplied that it can resort to its best judgment.
The Respondent is, however, not at liberty to invoke and
apply its best judgment option arbitrarily and in a manner
that suits it.”
62. Just like it was in the Superserve case (supra), the Appellant failed to
supply the Respondent with relevant documents that it was obliged to
provide in support of its objection. The Respondent was therefore at liberty
to invoke and apply its best judgment to determine the Appellant’s fair tax
liability.
DISPOSITION
63. In the upshot, the Tribunal hereby orders as follows:
Judgment TAT No. E142 of 2025 (Ainushamsi Construction and Transporters Limited vs
Commissioner of Investigations and Enforcement)
Page 14 of 15

a. The Appeal is hereby dismissed.
b. The Review Decision dated 24th December 2024 be and is hereby
upheld.
c. Each party to bear its own costs.
64. It is so ordered.
DATED and DELIVERED at NAIROBI this …..….26TH……. day of……
MARCH........ 2026
..........................……………………….
DR. RODNEY ODHIAMBO OLUOCH
CHAIRPERSON
….……………………………… …………………………..
DR. ERICK KOMOLO CAROLINE WESONGA
MEMBER MEMBER
Judgment TAT No. E142 of 2025 (Ainushamsi Construction and Transporters Limited vs
Commissioner of Investigations and Enforcement)
Page 15 of 15
